q: The imaginary line around which the Earth rotates is called its:
sol: Earth rotates on its axis, which is tilted at 23.5 degrees.

q: How long does it take for the Earth to complete one rotation?
sol: One rotation takes approximately 24 hours, causing day and night.

q: The ancient instrument used to tell time by the position of the sun's shadow is a:
sol: Sundials were among the earliest devices used to track time during the day.

q: The study of celestial objects and phenomena is known as:
sol: Astronomy is the scientific study of stars, planets, and the universe.

q: Which star remains almost stationary in the sky because it lies on the Earth's axis of rotation?
sol: The Pole Star stays fixed above the North Pole, helping in navigation.

q: The time taken by the Earth to revolve around the Sun once is called a:
sol: A solar year is approximately 365.25 days.

q: The changing shapes of the visible part of the moon are called:
sol: Phases occur because we see different amounts of the moon's sunlit side.

q: A group of stars forming a recognizable pattern is called a:
sol: Constellations like Ursa Major help identify regions of the sky.

q: Which constellation is also known as the Great Bear?
sol: Ursa Major contains the famous 'Big Dipper' asterism.

q: The phase of the moon when it is completely dark from Earth is the:
sol: During New Moon, the moon is between the Earth and the Sun.

q: The interval between one New Moon and the next is approximately:
sol: A synodic month (New Moon to New Moon) is about 29.5 days.

q: In which direction does the Sun appear to rise?
sol: Because Earth rotates West to East, the Sun appears to rise in the East.

q: Which Indian astronomer proposed that the Earth is round and rotates on its axis?
sol: Aryabhata was a pioneer in describing Earth's rotation in ancient India.

q: The path followed by a planet around the sun is called its:
sol: Orbits are generally elliptical (oval-shaped).

q: Which constellation is shaped like a hunter?
sol: Orion is easily recognized by the three stars forming 'Orion's Belt'.

q: An extra day added to the month of February every four years creates a:
sol: Leap years account for the extra quarter-day in Earth's orbit.

q: The moon does not have its own light; it reflects light from the:
sol: The moon is a non-luminous body that reflects sunlight.

q: When more than half of the moon is visible, it is called a ________ moon.
sol: Gibbous comes from a word meaning 'humped'.

q: Which of these is used to track months in the Indian lunar calendar?
sol: A Tithi is a lunar day in the Hindu calendar.

q: The point in the sky directly above the observer is called the:
sol: Zenith is the highest point relative to an observer's position.

q: The 'Seven Sages' constellation is known in India as:
sol: Saptarishi is the Indian name for Ursa Major.

q: In which season is the constellation Orion clearly visible in the evening?
sol: Orion is a prominent winter constellation.

q: A 'waxing' moon means the visible portion is:
sol: Waxing refers to the period from New Moon to Full Moon.

q: The sun is a ________.
sol: The sun is a medium-sized star at the center of our solar system.

q: Ancient people used the movement of ________ to track seasons for farming.
sol: Predicting seasons was vital for agriculture in early civilizations.

q: Which constellation looks like a distorted letter 'W' or 'M'?
sol: Cassiopeia is located in the northern sky.

q: The day of the Full Moon is called ________ in India.
sol: Purnima is the night when the entire side of the moon facing Earth is lit.

q: A 'waning' moon means the visible portion is:
sol: Waning happens from Full Moon back to New Moon.

q: Stars appear to move from ________ to ________ in the night sky.
sol: This is due to the Earth's rotation from West to East.

q: Which planet is known as the 'Morning Star' or 'Evening Star'?
sol: Venus is highly reflective and appears bright near sunrise or sunset.

q: The distance light travels in one year is called a:
sol: It is a unit of distance used for the vast space between stars.

q: What is the approximate speed of light?
sol: Light travels incredibly fast, covering 3 lakh kilometers every second.

q: Which star is closest to our Solar System (after the Sun)?
sol: It is about 4.2 light-years away from Earth.

q: How many major planets are there in our solar system?
sol: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.

q: The sun's diameter is about ________ times that of Earth.
sol: The sun is massive compared to all the planets.

q: Shooting stars are actually:
sol: Meteors are space rocks burning up in Earth's atmosphere.

q: Halley's Comet appears after every ________ years.
sol: It was last seen in 1986 and will return in 2061.

q: The largest planet in the solar system is:
sol: Jupiter is so large that over 1300 Earths could fit inside it.

q: A planet that has a prominent ring system is:
sol: Saturn's rings are made of ice and dust particles.

q: The red planet is:
sol: Mars appears red due to iron oxide (rust) on its surface.

q: Which of these is a natural satellite of Earth?
sol: The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite.

q: Celestial objects that revolve around planets are called:
sol: Satellites can be natural (moons) or man-made.

q: The 'Belt of Asteroids' is located between the orbits of:
sol: This belt contains millions of rocky objects.

q: Which planet is the coldest and farthest from the sun?
sol: Neptune is the eighth and outermost planet.

q: A 'Blue Moon' refers to:
o: The second full moon in a month
sol: It is a rare calendar event occurring once every 2-3 years.

q: The tilt of Earth's axis causes:
sol: The tilt affects how much sunlight different regions receive throughout the year.

q: The first man-made satellite launched by India was:
sol: Aryabhata was launched in 1975.

q: Celestial bodies with a long glowing tail are:
sol: Comets develop tails when they get close to the sun.

q: Which of the following does NOT belong to the solar system?
sol: Constellations are distant patterns of stars, not part of our local system.

q: The bright streak of light caused by a meteoroid falling through the atmosphere is a:
sol: Friction with air makes the meteoroid glow hot.

q: If a meteor reaches the Earth's surface, it is called a:
sol: Meteorites are the remnants that survive the atmospheric entry.

q: Which planet rotates on its axis from East to West?
sol: Venus has a retrograde rotation, unlike most other planets.

q: A light-year measures ________.
sol: It is the distance light travels in one year (approx. 9.46 trillion km).

q: Which constellation contains the star Sirius?
sol: Sirius is the brightest star in the night sky.

q: The darkest part of a shadow is called the:
sol: The umbra is where the light source is completely blocked.

q: During a lunar eclipse, the ________ comes between the Sun and the Moon.
sol: Earth's shadow falls on the moon.

q: A solar eclipse occurs only on a ________ day.
sol: The moon must be between the Sun and the Earth.

q: Which of these planets has no moon?
sol: Mercury and Venus are the only planets in our system without moons.

q: The galaxy we live in is called the:
sol: In India, it is often called 'Akash Ganga'.

q: Which planet has the shortest day (fastest rotation)?
sol: Jupiter rotates once in just under 10 hours.

q: Tides in the ocean are primarily caused by the gravitational pull of the:
sol: The moon's gravity pulls on Earth's water, creating high and low tides.

q: The sun appears to move 15 degrees every hour. This is used to define:
sol: Since 360 / 24 = 15, each hour corresponds to 15 degrees of longitude.

q: What is the primary gas found in the Sun?
sol: The Sun is mostly hydrogen undergoing nuclear fusion into helium.

q: Which planet is known for its 'Great Red Spot'?
sol: The spot is a massive storm that has lasted for centuries.

q: The surface of the moon is covered with dark flat plains called:
sol: These are basaltic plains formed by ancient volcanic eruptions.

q: The tilt of the Earth's axis is ________ degrees.
sol: This tilt is responsible for the distribution of heat across latitudes.

q: Which ancient civilization built the Stonehenge to track celestial events?
sol: Stonehenge was likely used as a calendar and observatory.

q: A leap year has ________ days.
sol: The extra day is February 29th.

q: Which planet is often called Earth's twin?
sol: Venus has a similar size and mass to Earth.

q: The 'Hunters' moon usually occurs in:
sol: It is the full moon following the Harvest Moon.

q: The time between two consecutive Full Moons is about ________ days.
sol: This period is known as the synodic month.

q: Which planet has the longest year (revolution time)?
sol: Neptune takes about 165 Earth years to orbit the Sun.

q: Planets reflect light, while stars ________ light.
sol: Stars are luminous bodies that produce energy via fusion.

q: The Earth is ________ from the Sun in winter (in Northern Hemisphere).
sol: Seasons are caused by tilt, not distance from the sun.

q: A person who travels into space is an:
sol: Astronauts work in space; astronomers study it from Earth.

q: The first human to set foot on the moon was:
sol: Neil Armstrong landed on the moon in 1969.

q: The boundary between day and night on the moon is the:
sol: The terminator line moves as the moon rotates.

q: Which of these is NOT a phase of the moon?
sol: Oval is not a standard astronomical name for a moon phase.

q: What prevents planets from flying off into space?
sol: The Sun's gravity keeps all planets in their orbits.

q: The Equinox occurs when:
sol: This happens twice a year, in March and September.

q: Which planet is the least dense (would float in water)?
sol: Saturn is mostly gas and has a very low density.

q: A solar day is ________ minutes longer than a sidereal day.
sol: Because Earth moves in orbit, it must rotate slightly more each day to face the sun.

q: The sun’s light takes about ________ to reach Earth.
sol: Light travels 150 million km in about 8 minutes and 20 seconds.

q: Which constellation is used to find the North Star?
sol: The 'pointers' in Ursa Major point toward Polaris.

q: The moon’s gravity is about ________ of Earth’s gravity.
sol: This is why astronauts can jump very high on the moon.

q: A 'New Moon' occurs when the moon is located ________.
sol: The side facing us is not lit by the sun.

q: Indian calendar months like Chaitra and Vaishakha are based on:
sol: The Indian national calendar is lunisolar.

q: The outermost layer of the Sun is called the:
sol: The corona is visible during a total solar eclipse.

q: The North Star is always visible in the ________ sky (in Northern Hemisphere).
sol: It sits directly above the North Pole.

q: Planets that are primarily made of rock are called:
sol: Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars are terrestrial planets.

q: How many Earths could fit inside the Sun?
sol: The Sun's volume is vast.

q: The study of how celestial bodies affect human lives is:
sol: Astrology is a belief system, while astronomy is a science.

q: Which star is also called the 'Dog Star'?
sol: It is the brightest star in Canis Major.

q: The phase of the moon where only a tiny sliver is visible is:
sol: Crescents appear just before or after a New Moon.

q: The Summer Solstice is the ________ day of the year.
sol: In the Northern Hemisphere, it occurs around June 21.

q: In ancient India, hours were measured in ________.
sol: One Ghatika is roughly 24 minutes.

q: Which planet has a surface pressure 90 times that of Earth?
sol: Venus has a thick, toxic atmosphere.

q: The 'Great Bear' constellation appears in the ________ sky.
sol: It is a circumpolar constellation in the North.

q: The light from the moon takes ________ to reach Earth.
sol: Since the moon is close (384,000 km), light reaches us almost instantly.

q: The solar system belongs to the ________ Galaxy.
sol: Our home galaxy contains billions of stars.
